rationale: DORA Art.5(1) mandates an internal ICT governance/control framework for prudent ICT risk management; ISO 27005 cl.5 defines the information security risk management process that operationalises such a framework.
DORA requires the management body to bear ultimate ICT risk responsibility; ISO 27005 cl.10.2 addresses leadership commitment and accountability for the risk management process.

DORA requires policies for data availability, authenticity, integrity, and confidentiality; ISO 27005 cl.6.4.3.2 defines consequence criteria that must capture impacts on those same security properties.
DORA requires clear roles/responsibilities for ICT functions; ISO 27005 cl.6.1 requires establishing the organisational context including roles and responsibilities for the risk management process.

DORA mandates setting and approving a digital operational resilience strategy including risk tolerance; ISO 27005 cl.6.4 is broader, requiring establishment and maintenance of all risk criteria including acceptance criteria and assessment criteria.
DORA requires the management body to approve and review ICT business continuity and response/recovery plans; ISO 27005 cl.8.6 covers risk treatment planning and approval by risk owners.
DORA requires the management body to approve and review ICT internal audit plans; ISO 27005 cl.10.6 covers management review of the risk management process.
DORA requires periodic budget allocation for digital resilience; ISO 27005 cl.10.2 addresses leadership providing necessary resources for the risk management process.
DORA requires a policy on use of ICT third-party service providers; ISO 27005 cl.6.2 requires identifying requirements of interested parties, which includes supply-chain/third-party context.
DORA requires reporting channels for third-party arrangements and major incidents; ISO 27005 cl.10.3 covers communication and consultation as a supporting ISMS process.
DORA requires a dedicated function/role to monitor third-party ICT relationships; ISO 27005 cl.7.2.2 requires identifying risk owners who are accountable for identified risks.
DORA requires management body members to maintain ICT knowledge through training; ISO 27005 cl.10.2 addresses leadership competence and commitment to support the risk process.

DORA Art.6(1) requires a sound, comprehensive, well-documented ICT risk management framework integrated into overall risk management; ISO 27005 cl.5 defines the equivalent comprehensive information security risk management process.

DORA requires the framework to encompass strategies, policies, and tools protecting information and ICT assets; ISO 27005 cl.6.1 is broader, establishing full organisational context for risk management including asset scope.

DORA requires measures to reduce ICT risk impact and provide complete risk information to authorities; ISO 27005 cl.8.2 covers selecting appropriate risk treatment options to reduce risk to acceptable levels.
DORA requires independent ICT risk oversight following three-lines-of-defence; ISO 27005 cl.10.2 addresses leadership and independence in the risk management process.
DORA requires annual review of the framework with continuous improvement and updates following incidents; ISO 27005 cl.10.8 covers continual improvement of the risk management process.
DORA requires regular internal audit of the ICT risk framework by qualified auditors; ISO 27005 cl.10.6 covers management review which subsumes internal audit findings.
DORA requires formal follow-up and timely remediation of critical ICT audit findings; ISO 27005 cl.10.7 covers corrective action arising from identified nonconformities.

DORA requires the resilience strategy to explain how the framework supports business strategy; ISO 27005 cl.6.1 requires establishing organisational context by aligning risk management with business objectives.
DORA requires establishing risk tolerance and impact tolerance for ICT disruptions; ISO 27005 cl.6.4.2 directly specifies establishing risk acceptance criteria.
DORA requires clear information security objectives with KPIs and KRMs; ISO 27005 cl.6.4.3 establishes criteria for performing risk assessments including consequence and likelihood criteria.
DORA requires documenting ICT reference architecture; ISO 27005 cl.6.3 covers applying risk assessment scope which includes defining architecture boundaries.
DORA requires outlining detection and prevention mechanisms; ISO 27005 cl.7.2 covers identifying risks including threat/vulnerability identification that underpins detection and prevention design.
DORA requires evidencing current resilience situation via incident reporting metrics; ISO 27005 cl.9.1 covers performing the risk assessment process operationally to reflect current risk posture.

DORA requires implementing digital operational resilience testing per Chapter IV; ISO 27005 cl.9.1 covers performing the risk assessment process which provides the inputs tested controls are validated against.
DORA requires an incident communication strategy; ISO 27005 cl.10.3 addresses communication and consultation as an explicit ISMS process supporting risk management.
DORA allows documenting multi-vendor ICT strategies identifying third-party dependencies; ISO 27005 cl.6.2 requires identifying interested-party requirements including supply-chain dependencies.

DORA requires ICT systems to be appropriate to the magnitude of operations (proportionality); ISO 27005 cl.6.1 establishes the organisational context including scale and scope that determines proportionate risk controls.
DORA requires ICT systems to be reliable; ISO 27005 cl.8.3 covers determining necessary controls, including reliability controls, needed to treat identified risks.

DORA requires ICT systems to have sufficient capacity to handle peak transaction volumes; ISO 27005 cl.7.3.2 assesses potential consequences of risk materialisation including capacity exhaustion scenarios.
DORA requires ICT systems to be technologically resilient under stressed conditions; ISO 27005 cl.7.3.3 assesses likelihood including stressed-scenario likelihoods that drive resilience design.

DORA Art.8(1) requires identifying, classifying, and documenting all ICT-supported business functions and assets with yearly reviews; ISO 27005 cl.7.2.1 directly covers identifying and describing information security risks including asset identification.

DORA Art.8(2) requires continuous identification of ICT risk sources, cyber threats, vulnerabilities, and yearly risk scenario reviews; ISO 27005 cl.7.2.1 covers identifying and describing risks using asset-based or event-based approaches.

DORA Art.8(2) requires assessment of cyber threats and ICT vulnerabilities; ISO 27005 cl.7.3 covers analysing risks including assessing consequences and likelihood of threat-vulnerability combinations.

DORA Art.8(3) requires risk assessments following major infrastructure or procedural changes; ISO 27005 cl.9.1 covers performing the risk assessment process, including trigger conditions for reassessment.

DORA Art.8(4) requires identifying and mapping all information and ICT assets including remote sites and interdependencies; ISO 27005 cl.7.2.1 is broader, covering full risk identification including assets, threats, vulnerabilities, and impacts.

DORA Art.8(5) requires documenting third-party service provider dependencies and interconnections; ISO 27005 cl.6.2 identifies requirements of interested parties including supply-chain dependencies that are part of context.
DORA Art.8(5) requires mapping ICT processes reliant on third-party providers; ISO 27005 cl.7.2.1 risk identification includes identifying risks from third-party dependencies as a risk source.

DORA Art.8(6) requires maintaining and periodically updating relevant inventories; ISO 27005 cl.10.4 covers documented information requirements, including maintaining records of the risk assessment outputs.

DORA Art.8(7) requires yearly assessment of legacy ICT systems and evaluations before connecting new technologies; ISO 27005 cl.7.2.1 is broader, covering full risk identification applicable to legacy and new system scenarios.

DORA Art.9(1) requires continuous monitoring and minimisation of ICT risk impact through security tools and policies; ISO 27005 cl.8.2 covers selecting appropriate risk treatment options to minimise risk.

DORA Art.9(2) requires security measures for resilience, continuity, and availability across all data states; ISO 27005 cl.8.3 covers determining the specific controls necessary for implementing chosen treatment options.
DORA Art.9(3)(a) requires securing means of data transfer; ISO 27005 cl.8.3 is broader, covering determination of all controls needed to implement risk treatment including data-in-transit controls.
DORA Art.9(3)(b) requires reducing risks of data corruption, loss, and unauthorised entry; ISO 27005 cl.8.3 determines all necessary controls to treat risks including integrity and access controls.
DORA Art.9(3)(c) requires preventing unavailability, authenticity/integrity breaches, and data loss; ISO 27005 cl.8.3 covers control selection for CIA risk treatment which subsumes these objectives.
DORA Art.9(3)(d) requires protecting against management risks including human error; ISO 27005 cl.7.3.2 assesses potential consequences including those arising from human error and process failures.

DORA Art.9(4)(a) requires documented information security policies for data availability, authenticity, integrity, and confidentiality; ISO 27005 cl.8.3 determines necessary controls including policy-based controls.

DORA Art.9(4)(b) requires network management using automated isolation mechanisms for cyber-attacks; ISO 27005 cl.8.3 covers determining necessary technical controls including network segmentation and isolation.
DORA Art.9(4)(c) requires access limitation policies with appropriate controls; ISO 27005 cl.8.3 covers determining necessary access controls as part of risk treatment implementation.

DORA Art.9(4)(d) requires strong authentication and encryption based on risk assessments; ISO 27005 cl.8.3 specifies that controls must be determined based on the risk assessment outputs, which drives authentication and encryption strength.

DORA Art.9(4)(e) requires documented ICT change management policies for recorded and tested changes; ISO 27005 cl.8.6.1 covers formulation of the risk treatment plan which must account for changes in ICT environment.
DORA Art.9(4)(f) requires comprehensive patch and update procedures; ISO 27005 cl.8.3 covers determining necessary controls including vulnerability management controls.

DORA Art.10(1) requires mechanisms to promptly detect anomalous activities and material single points of failure; ISO 27005 cl.7.2.1 covers identifying risks including identifying single points of failure as risk scenarios.
DORA Art.10(1) requires ongoing detection mechanisms regularly tested; ISO 27005 cl.10.5 covers monitoring and review to ensure controls remain effective and risks are detected.

DORA Art.10(2) requires multi-layer controls, alert thresholds, and incident response triggers; ISO 27005 cl.6.4.3.4 establishes criteria for determining the level of risk including threshold criteria.
DORA Art.10(3) requires sufficient resources to monitor user activity, ICT anomalies, and cyber-attacks; ISO 27005 cl.10.5 covers performing monitoring and review of risk-related events.
DORA Art.11(1) requires an ICT business continuity policy as part of the risk framework; ISO 27005 cl.8.6.1 covers formulating the risk treatment plan which includes continuity-related controls.

DORA Art.11(2) requires documented arrangements ensuring continuity of critical functions through incident response and containment; ISO 27005 cl.8.6.1 covers formulating the risk treatment plan including incident response planning.
DORA Art.11(3) requires implementing ICT response and recovery plans with audit reviews; ISO 27005 cl.9.2 covers performing the risk treatment process operationally.

DORA Art.11(4) requires maintaining and periodically testing business continuity plans including outsourced critical functions; ISO 27005 cl.9.2 covers performing and validating risk treatment including continuity controls.

DORA Art.11(5) requires a business impact analysis evaluating severe disruption exposures using quantitative and qualitative methods; ISO 27005 cl.7.3.2 directly covers assessing potential consequences of risk materialisation.
DORA Art.11(5) requires BIA to determine risk levels for critical functions; ISO 27005 cl.7.3.4 covers determining levels of risk by combining consequence and likelihood assessments.

DORA Art.11(6) requires annual testing of continuity and crisis communication plans with review based on test results; ISO 27005 cl.9.1 covers performing risk assessments that must be triggered by test outcomes.
DORA Art.11(6) requires plans to be regularly reviewed and updated based on test results; ISO 27005 cl.10.8 covers continual improvement driven by review results.
DORA Art.11(7) requires a crisis management function with internal and external communication procedures; ISO 27005 cl.10.3 covers communication and consultation as a supporting ISMS process.
DORA Art.11(8) requires disruption event records to remain readily accessible; ISO 27005 cl.10.4 covers documented information requirements for recording risk management process results.

DORA Art.13(1) requires capabilities to gather and analyse information on vulnerabilities and cyber threats; ISO 27005 cl.7.2.1 covers identifying and describing risks, including threat intelligence as an input to identification.
DORA Art.13(2) requires post-incident reviews analysing disruption causes and identifying improvements; ISO 27005 cl.10.7 covers corrective action following identified nonconformities.
DORA Art.13(2)(a) requires evaluating response speed to security alerts; ISO 27005 cl.10.5 covers monitoring and review including evaluating effectiveness of risk controls.
DORA Art.13(2)(b) requires evaluating quality and speed of forensic analysis; ISO 27005 cl.10.5 covers reviewing whether risk monitoring processes delivered timely and accurate information.
DORA Art.13(2)(c) requires evaluating effectiveness of internal incident escalation; ISO 27005 cl.10.5 covers reviewing whether risk-related events were escalated appropriately.

DORA Art.13(2)(d) requires evaluating effectiveness of internal and external communication during incidents; ISO 27005 cl.10.3 covers the communication and consultation process used during risk events.

DORA Art.13(3) requires lessons from testing and incidents to be incorporated into ICT risk assessments and trigger framework reviews; ISO 27005 cl.9.1 covers performing risk assessments as an operational activity triggered by such events.
DORA Art.13(3) requires continual incorporation of lessons learned into the risk management framework; ISO 27005 cl.10.8 covers continual improvement driven by lessons learned and review outcomes.

DORA Art.13(4) requires monitoring resilience strategy effectiveness and analysing ICT incident frequency, types, and patterns; ISO 27005 cl.10.5 covers monitoring and review of risk-related events and risk management performance.

DORA Art.13(4) requires analysing incident patterns to understand risk exposure levels; ISO 27005 cl.7.4 covers evaluating risks by comparing analysis results with risk criteria to determine treatment priorities.
DORA Art.13(5) requires senior ICT staff to report yearly findings and recommendations to management bodies; ISO 27005 cl.10.6 covers management review of the risk management process.

DORA Art.13(6) requires mandatory ICT security awareness programs and resilience training for all staff; ISO 27005 cl.10.2 addresses leadership commitment including ensuring competence and awareness for risk management.

DORA Art.13(7) requires continuous monitoring of technological developments affecting ICT security; ISO 27005 cl.10.5 covers monitoring risk-related events including technology changes that alter the risk landscape.

DORA Art.16(1)(a) requires a simplified but sound and documented ICT risk management framework for microenterprises; ISO 27005 cl.5 defines the full information security risk management process, of which this is a proportionate subset.
DORA Art.16(1)(b) requires continuous monitoring of ICT system security and functionality; ISO 27005 cl.10.5 covers monitoring and review of risk-related events on an ongoing basis.

DORA Art.16(1)(c) requires sound, resilient, and updated ICT systems to minimise risk impact; ISO 27005 cl.8.3 covers determining necessary controls to treat risks including system resilience controls.

DORA Art.16(1)(d) requires prompt identification of ICT risk sources and swift incident handling; ISO 27005 cl.7.2.1 covers identifying and describing information security risks including risk source identification.

DORA Art.16(1)(e) requires identifying dependencies on third-party ICT service providers; ISO 27005 cl.6.2 covers identifying requirements and dependencies of interested parties including third parties.

DORA Art.16(1)(f) requires business continuity and recovery plans for critical function continuity; ISO 27005 cl.8.6.1 covers formulating the risk treatment plan including continuity-based risk treatment.
DORA Art.16(1)(g) requires regular testing of plans and control effectiveness; ISO 27005 cl.9.2 covers performing the risk treatment process including verification of control effectiveness.

DORA Art.16(1)(h) requires implementing testing conclusions into risk assessments and developing security awareness programs; ISO 27005 cl.10.8 covers continual improvement driven by operational results.

DORA Art.16(2) requires documented frameworks that are periodically reviewed with continuous improvement; ISO 27005 cl.10.4 covers documented information requirements for recording and maintaining risk management artefacts.
DORA Art.16(2) requires frameworks to improve continuously; ISO 27005 cl.10.8 covers continual improvement as a foundational ISMS process.
DORA Art.8(2) requires at least yearly review of risk scenarios; ISO 27005 cl.7.4 covers evaluating risks to prioritise treatment, which feeds the periodic scenario review cycle.
DORA Art.9(1) requires continuously minimising ICT risk through security tools; ISO 27005 cl.8.1 provides the general risk treatment framework that governs selection and deployment of such tools.
DORA Art.11(5) BIA must determine which disruptions are severe enough to require treatment action; ISO 27005 cl.7.4.2 covers prioritising risks for treatment using risk evaluation results.

DORA Art.13(4) requires comparing observed incident patterns against risk exposure levels; ISO 27005 cl.7.4.1 covers comparing risk analysis results with risk criteria to determine whether risks are acceptable.
DORA Art.6(5) requires annual review of the ICT risk framework including post-incident updates; ISO 27005 cl.10.6 covers management review which drives framework updates.

DORA Art.8(4) requires identification and mapping of all ICT assets and interdependencies as scope inputs; ISO 27005 cl.6.3 covers applying risk assessment scope including defining what is in scope for assessment.

DORA Art.10(2) requires defining alert thresholds to trigger incident response; ISO 27005 cl.7.3.4 covers determining risk levels by combining consequence and likelihood, which underpins threshold calibration.

DORA Art.9(2) requires high standards of data protection resilience across all data states; ISO 27005 cl.8.2 covers selecting appropriate risk treatment options including reduce/avoid/transfer/retain decisions.
DORA Art.6(8)(b) requires determining impact tolerance for ICT disruptions; ISO 27005 cl.8.6.3 covers formal acceptance of residual information security risks after treatment.
DORA Art.11(3) requires ICT response and recovery plans with audit reviews and approval; ISO 27005 cl.8.6.2 covers approval of the risk treatment plan by risk owners.

DORA Art.6(3) requires providing complete and updated risk information to authorities upon request; ISO 27005 cl.10.4 covers documented information including results that must be maintained and accessible.

DORA Art.13(1) requires analysing vulnerability and threat information to assess effects on resilience; ISO 27005 cl.7.3.3 covers assessing likelihood of risk scenarios based on threat and vulnerability intelligence.
DORA Art.5(2)(d) requires the management body to set the risk tolerance level for ICT risk; ISO 27005 cl.6.4.2 directly requires establishing risk acceptance criteria including tolerance thresholds.

DORA Art.8(7) requires risk assessments before/after connecting new technologies and yearly legacy reviews; ISO 27005 cl.9.1 covers the trigger conditions for performing risk assessments, including technology change events.
DORA Art.13(2) post-incident reviews must feed into the ICT risk assessment framework; ISO 27005 cl.9.1 covers performing risk assessments as an operational activity triggered by incidents.

DORA Art.16(1)(d) requires swift incident handling and prompt risk source identification for microenterprises; ISO 27005 cl.10.5 covers monitoring risk-related events to enable prompt detection and response.

DORA Art.6(4) requires an independent control function following three-lines-of-defence; ISO 27005 cl.6.3 covers applying the risk assessment including defining organisational roles and independence for the process.

DORA Art.9(4)(d) requires authentication and encryption to be based on standards and risk assessments; ISO 27005 cl.7.4 covers evaluating risk results against criteria, which determines the appropriate strength of cryptographic controls.
DORA Art.11(6) requires cyber-attack scenarios and infrastructure switchover drills in testing; ISO 27005 cl.7.4 covers evaluating risks including prioritising which scenarios warrant testing.

DORA Art.13(3) requires supervisory review information to inform risk assessments; ISO 27005 cl.7.2 covers identifying information security risks using all available intelligence including external supervisory inputs.
